meta.title: Realism scorecard element catalog
meta.note: Canonical per-element checklist. Regenerate scorecard.html via code/build_scorecard.py after editing.

elements.label: Multi-segment graph with enforced path behavior
elements.desc: Multiple zones (DMZ, user, server, admin, OT) with per-protocol/per-segment ACLs; scans, sessions, blocked flows, and deny decisions follow routing, segmentation, and policy rather than global reachability; defender isolation and policy changes alter reachable paths measurably from network vantage points.

elements.label: Perimeter and egress asymmetry
elements.desc: Distinction between externally-exposed and internal-only services; differentiated outbound policy, proxy/DNS egress filtering, and multiple distinct egress channels with different detection profiles.

elements.label: Network devices as manageable entities
elements.desc: Routers, switches, firewalls with CLI/SNMP/configs; configuration-change events observable; supports network-device persistence and traffic-signaling primitives.

elements.label: Layer 2/3 protocol fidelity
elements.desc: Real ARP, DHCP, LLMNR/NBT-NS, SMB signing, 802.1X with their real weaknesses (poisoning, relay, NTLM capture) and countermeasures.

elements.label: Position-dependent traffic visibility
elements.desc: What can be sniffed or MITM'd depends on attacker location; SPAN/mirroring possible only where topology supports it; defender visibility depends on sensor placement and monitored paths, not global access.

elements.label: Lateral reachability and file transfer
elements.desc: SMB admin shares, SCP/SFTP, WinRM, RDP drive mapping function only where topology allows; transfers generate realistic internal flows.

elements.label: Discoverable (not given) topology
elements.desc: Scans return partial, segmentation-dependent, and noisy results; topology must be earned through discovery rather than handed to the agent.

elements.label: Staging and exfil-path asymmetry
elements.desc: Hosts differ in external connectivity, forcing realistic staging decisions and supporting internal-to-edge data movement.

elements.label: Remote-access and cross-organizational boundaries
elements.desc: VPN concentrators, jump hosts, vendor/MSP access, federated reachability as boundary-crossing mechanisms with their own auth and logging.

elements.label: Believable decoy placement
elements.desc: Decoys occupy plausible network locations, segments, routes, and exposure patterns rather than obviously isolated trap zones.

elements.label: Real, versioned, fingerprintable daemons
elements.desc: SSH, RDP, SMB, HTTP(S), LDAP, databases, mail, WinRM, DCOM actually listening with protocol-conformant responses, banners, and version-specific quirks; services evaluate access requests at their own decision points using identity, session, policy, and resource state.

elements.label: Exploitable vulnerable builds with heterogeneous patch state
elements.desc: Version-specific vulnerabilities (EternalBlue, BlueKeep, Log4Shell, PrintNightmare, Zerologon) with real exploit parameters; patch state inconsistent across hosts.

elements.label: Web applications with real code paths and session stack
elements.desc: Exploitable vulns (SQLi, SSRF, RCE, deserialization), realistic directory structures, cookies with HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite, OAuth/OIDC/SAML flows with forge-able tokens when keys are compromised; session validity, token expiry, and MFA enforced on high-risk flows.

elements.label: Email infrastructure
elements.desc: SMTP/IMAP/Exchange/Graph with working flow, real mailboxes, SPF/DKIM/DMARC enforceable, mail-rule creation as an abusable primitive.

elements.label: DNS infrastructure
elements.desc: Authoritative DNS with full record set, realistic TTL/NXDOMAIN behavior, observable egress queries, enough fidelity to support fast-flux and DGA analysis.

elements.label: File shares and enterprise repositories
elements.desc: SMB/NFS/DFS shares with realistic ACLs and content; Confluence/SharePoint/wiki with realistic org content; code repos with accidental secrets; shares and libraries evaluate role-and-resource permissions before returning content.

elements.label: Authentication-service and PKI surfaces
elements.desc: Services validate password attempts realistically for spray/brute force with lockout/throttling/MFA per policy; captured hashes in realistic formats; ADCS/CA with abusable templates and client-cert authentication.

elements.label: Network-device management services
elements.desc: SNMP with guessable communities, device CLI, TFTP/FTP config backups containing real architectural data.

elements.label: Enterprise deployment and supply-chain services
elements.desc: SCCM/MECM/Ansible with agents on endpoints, package managers and registries, CI/CD pipelines producing signed artifacts, update mechanisms with modeled trust relationships.

elements.label: Legitimate RMM and dual-use web services
elements.desc: TeamViewer/AnyDesk/ScreenConnect installable and functional; cloud storage, code repos, messaging APIs reachable as potential covert-C2 channels. Their legitimacy is what defeats naive detection.

elements.label: Revocation-aware service state
elements.desc: Services, token consumers, session caches, and application connections react to credential revocation and restoration according to their own session and cache semantics.

elements.label: Operational recovery surfaces
elements.desc: Backup, restore, rollback, and recovery exist as operational service surfaces with realistic targets, dependencies, permissions, and workflow steps.

elements.label: Dependency-aware response effects
elements.desc: Service dependencies react meaningfully when hardening, isolation, eviction, or restore actions affect required identity/network/storage/database/application dependencies.

elements.label: Believable decoy service context
elements.desc: Decoy services expose plausible protocols, data, workflows, and dependencies for the business context they imitate and behave consistently with nearby legitimate services.

elements.label: Persistence surface
elements.desc: Autostart, scheduled execution, services/daemons, and event-triggered execution behave per spec, survive reboot, and emit telemetry on modification; the same primitives are the inspectable, harden-able, restore-able host-state objects. Windows (Run keys, services, Task Scheduler, WMI subscriptions), Linux (systemd, cron, init.d, rc, udev), macOS (launchd, LoginItems, emond).

elements.label: Execution-flow control and scripting engines
elements.desc: Loader/module-resolution abuse (DLL search order, LD_PRELOAD, dyld, COM hijack), signed-binary proxy execution and LOLBins, and real scripting engines with engine-level telemetry; hosts provide local OS-native enforcement points (allowlisting, syscall filtering, local firewall).

elements.label: Process, memory, and privilege-boundary primitives
elements.desc: Process injection, token manipulation/impersonation, reflective/in-memory loading, IPC endpoints (named pipes, DCOM, D-Bus, XPC), and real elevation boundaries (UAC, sudo/setuid, TCC, polkit) with bypass paths; killing a payload may be undone by a service manager or scheduled task; protected-process termination requires authority and may fail.

elements.label: Configuration stores, filesystem semantics, and kernel/trust layer
elements.desc: OS-native config surfaces (registry, /etc, plists) with ACLs/auditing; filesystem features (NTFS ADS, reparse points, Unix modes/xattrs/immutable flag, direct-volume access, timestomp); kernel/driver surface (signed-driver path, BYOVD, rootkit hooks), code-signing trust, and boot/platform trust (Secure Boot, TPM) exposed and verifiable.

elements.label: Credential material exposure on the endpoint
elements.desc: Credentials live where the real OS puts them and are harvestable through realistic paths: LSASS/SAM/DPAPI/cached logons/LSA secrets/Kerberos tickets; /etc/shadow and keyring; Keychain; plus browsers, SSH keys, cloud-CLI configs.

elements.label: Discovery, evasion, and anti-forensics surface
elements.desc: Host inventory queries return consistent, realistically large results; VM/debugger/time-based evasion checks and guardrails return plausible answers; anti-forensics primitives (hidden files/users, log/history clearing) work as the real OS would, with their own tell-tale artifacts.

elements.label: Impact primitives and document-execution behavior
elements.desc: Destructive actions (file encryption, shadow-copy deletion, disk wipe, service stop, reboot) and document-based execution paths (Office macros, PDF JS, LNK/ISO containers) behave per real OS semantics.

elements.label: Restorable host objects
elements.desc: Files, binaries, packages, disks, configuration, and system state exist as objects that can be compared to a known-good source and restored through a defender workflow with verifiable integrity.

elements.label: Populated directory and local identity stores
elements.desc: AD/LDAP/FreeIPA with realistic users, groups, OUs, nested memberships, SPNs, and misconfigured ACLs; local SAM, /etc/passwd-shadow, and cached domain credentials distributed non-uniformly across hosts.

elements.label: Credential landscape with realistic material in real locations
elements.desc: Varied password strength, reuse, default and service-account credentials; browser stores, keychain, SSH keys, cloud-CLI tokens, GPP cpassword, unattend.xml, shell history accumulating over time through actual use.

elements.label: Kerberos and NTLM protocol fidelity
elements.desc: AS/TGS exchanges with encryption negotiation, Kerberoasting/AS-REP roasting/S4U/PKINIT; NTLMv1/v2 challenge-response with relay to SMB/LDAP/HTTP/ADCS; legacy poisonable protocols (LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS).

elements.label: Federation, SSO, and web session material
elements.desc: SAML IdP with signing keys, OAuth/OIDC flows with access/refresh tokens, JWTs with claims, cross-tenant federation; web session cookies and API tokens whose theft enables authenticated replay; a single login establishes coherent IdP, directory, workstation, and service session state.

elements.label: MFA as a design space and auth-process modification surface
elements.desc: Multiple factor types (TOTP, SMS, push, FIDO2, smart-card), push-fatigue with a modeled user, per-app/conditional-access enforcement gaps; auth-package/SSP/PAM/skeleton-key modification paths; MFA outcomes recorded with user and source context.

elements.label: Account lifecycle and tiered administration
elements.desc: Create/enable/disable/reset/group-change primitives with logged artifacts; Tier-0/1/2 separation, LAPS-unique vs uniform local admin, MSA/gMSA vs plaintext service accounts.

elements.label: Session reality and valid-account usage
elements.desc: Distinguishable logon types (interactive, network, RDP); authenticated sessions with cached tickets and hashes in LSASS; runas/S4U/sudo/su produce real events enabling token theft and session hijacking.

elements.label: Certificate and PKI identity
elements.desc: ADCS with template misconfigurations (ESC1-ESC15), client-certificate authentication accepted by services, issuance/renewal/revocation events observable, code-signing and S/MIME certs as distinct surfaces.

elements.label: Enumeration and identity-relationship surface
elements.desc: net user/Get-ADUser/LDAP/BloodHound-style graph extraction return realistically large, internally consistent users, groups, sessions, SPNs, ACLs, trusts, tiering relationships, and account-management state reflecting the populated identity system.

elements.label: Human-layer impersonation and external-to-internal bridges
elements.desc: Email spoofing across SPF/DKIM/DMARC-enforced domains, internal spearphishing from compromised mailboxes, lookalike-domain impersonation; vendor/MSP accounts, B2B guests, service-principal identities as distinct populations with delegated rights.

elements.label: Policy-bound authorization as identity-layer state
elements.desc: Access to admin shares, SaaS resources, or sensitive APIs is derived from group membership, token claims, role assignments, or conditional-access policy at the identity layer (not static host-local lists); the policy's meaning and lifecycle are first-class identity state.

elements.label: Propagating credential control and access recovery
elements.desc: Credential/account state changes (revocation, invalidation, reissue, unlock, restoration) propagate with realistic scope, timing, and residual state; operational recovery workflows (review then unlock then reissue then restored membership then validation) exist as defender actions.

elements.label: Believable deceptive identities
elements.desc: Decoy accounts, credentials, tokens, or personas fit surrounding naming conventions, roles, privileges, and placement context with believable metadata and access scope.

elements.label: Real wall-clock progression
elements.desc: Continuous or fine-grained time (not per-action turns); monotonic timestamps on events, files, logs; consistent uptime/reboot history; NTP/time-source manipulation as a modeled primitive; event history preserved with ordering.

elements.label: Scheduled execution and reboot semantics
elements.desc: cron/Task Scheduler/systemd timers fire on configured schedule (not agent step); jitter expressible; reboots happen and clear non-persistent footholds; persistence, revocation, and restore state survive across reboot/reset boundaries to affect later actions.

elements.label: Session, token, and credential lifetimes
elements.desc: Kerberos TGT/renewal windows enforced; OAuth access/refresh tokens, cookies, certs, password-age/lockout counters all expire and reset on real elapsed time.

elements.label: Human activity rhythms
elements.desc: Business hours vs off-hours distinguishable in authentication and traffic baselines; weekday/weekend/holiday variation; shift patterns creating detection-latency variability.

elements.label: Long-horizon engagement support
elements.desc: Simulated time can span weeks or months; dwell semantics hold (T0 foothold persists to T+30d); logs accumulate, patches drift; enables long-dwell espionage, evasive red team, and threat hunting.

elements.label: Event-correlation windows and rate-limiting
elements.desc: SIEM rolling-window rules fire on real timing, historical retention is finite; account lockout, API 429s, connection-rate caps enforced in real time; brute-force must pace within limits.

elements.label: Delay-based evasion and staged operations
elements.desc: Sleep/NtDelayExecution actually suspend; time-based guardrails and long-delay detonation have real effect; multi-stage C2, scheduled transfer, low-and-slow exfil operate on real inter-stage delays.

elements.label: State decay and temporal detection signals
elements.desc: DNS/ARP/credential/Kerberos caches age on real TTLs; logs rotate, shadow copies age, certs approach expiry; unusual timing (3 AM logon, weekend transfer), beaconing cadence, dormant-account reactivation surface as anomalies.

elements.label: Non-instant defender effects with observable before/after
elements.desc: Revocation, containment, eviction, and recovery unfold over time; credential revocation disables fresh sessions immediately and cached/derived tokens only after expiry; defenders can observe when quarantine takes effect and whether a restored service stays stable.

elements.label: Retained containment and recovery history
elements.desc: Prior isolation, unlock, restore, and config-rollback actions remain visible during later investigation; the timeline includes when the response fired and what state it changed, supporting after-the-fact verification.

elements.label: Perimeter, egress, and traffic inspection
elements.desc: Stateful firewalls, WAF/IPS, web proxies with TLS inspection, DNS filtering, CASB; rate limiting and connection-state caps on exposed services.

elements.label: Email security stack
elements.desc: Mail gateway with attachment sandboxing and URL detonation, SPF/DKIM/DMARC enforcement, mailbox auditing with forwarding-rule alerts, internal-send anomaly detection.

elements.label: Endpoint protection, EDR, and exploit mitigations
elements.desc: AV + AMSI + behavioral; usermode API hooks and kernel telemetry; tamper protection; DEP/ASLR/CFG/CET, kernel mitigations, Credential Guard/LSA Protection/VBS; container/sandbox policies.

elements.label: Application-execution controls
elements.desc: AppLocker/WDAC/SRP/Gatekeeper/fapolicyd with tunable policy, script-engine controls (ExecutionPolicy, Constrained Language Mode), Office macro policies, SmartScreen/Gatekeeper prompts.

elements.label: Authentication hardening
elements.desc: Account lockout/smart-lockout/CAPTCHA; MFA via conditional access with realistic bypass gaps; NTLM restriction, SMB signing, LDAP channel binding, Kerberos-only enforcement; federation hardening (token binding, step-up, device compliance).

elements.label: Patching and configuration hardening
elements.desc: Patching modeled as ongoing, partial, time-varying; heterogeneous per-host patch state with permanent EOL subset; CIS-style baselines applied non-uniformly; legacy-protocol disablement as per-host config.

elements.label: Segmentation enforcement
elements.desc: Firewalls, host-based firewalls, VLAN ACLs, microsegmentation actively enforce reachability with both block and log; east-west inspection; zero-trust/identity-aware gateways as enforcement points.

elements.label: Detection rule library and cloud-plane controls
elements.desc: Sysmon/EDR/SIEM signature and behavioral rules with tunable coverage gaps; UEBA baselines over benign activity; IAM policies/SCPs/RBAC restricting API actions; CSPM/GuardDuty/Defender for Cloud.

elements.label: Data-loss prevention controls
elements.desc: Content-inspection DLP on endpoint, email, web, and cloud channels with volume-anomaly, archive-encryption, and sensitive-data classification heuristics; DLP can block, warn, quarantine, or alert on suspicious data movement.

elements.label: Response, deception, and control-configuration action space
elements.desc: Isolation/quarantine/revocation with realistic propagation delay and false-positive cost; honey accounts/files/tokens; controls are imperfect (false positives/negatives via obfuscation/LOLBins/BYOVD/AMSI bypass) and tamperable; defender capability is scoped; defenders can deploy/tune/push detections with rollout latency; containment/recovery carry operational side effects and a verification burden.
elements.anchors: T1531, T1685, T1027, T1218, UC-D2, UC-D4, UC-D5; D3-DE, D3-DO, D3-CE, D3-PE, D3-RA, D3-RO, D3-NI, D3-AMED, D3-MFA, D3-PH

elements.label: Simulated users with role and behavioral diversity
elements.desc: Multiple personas (developer, HR, finance, exec, sysadmin, helpdesk) with distinct application profiles, working hours, and access patterns; realistic workforce distribution (remote, mobile, shared).

elements.label: User interactive input and active sessions
elements.desc: Real keyboard/mouse events for keyloggers to capture; clipboard used for legitimate copy/paste; screen content, active windows, console/RDP/SSH sessions that exist because usage happened and are therefore hijackable.

elements.label: Browsing, document authorship, and accumulated web state
elements.desc: Users navigate real sites and authenticate to web apps; browser password stores, cookies, autofill accumulate over time; role-appropriate documents land where real work would put them; MRU lists and author metadata reflect real activity.

elements.label: Email activity
elements.desc: Realistic send/receive volumes, accumulated mailbox content, PST/OST files, calendar and distribution-list traffic, external correspondents for phishing ingress/egress.

elements.label: Credential-use traffic and lived-in accumulation
elements.desc: Users authenticate repeatedly, generating Kerberos/NTLM/SSO exchanges observable on the wire; users type passwords into prompts; shell history, known_hosts, DPAPI keys, cached credentials exist because users have actually logged on.

elements.label: Administrative and service baseline
elements.desc: Sysadmins run PowerShell/WMI/PsExec/SSH/Ansible for real reasons creating LotL cover; scheduled maintenance, patching, backups, updater check-ins, telemetry agents, MDM/SCCM on realistic cadences; LOLBin use has a legitimate baseline.

elements.label: Network and data-volume baselines
elements.desc: Realistic east-west traffic from shares, mail, printing, AD replication; outbound web, SaaS, updates, telemetry; DNS distribution supporting anomaly detection; per-user/per-destination upload-volume baselines.

elements.label: Share, repository, and peripheral activity
elements.desc: Shared-drive and Confluence/SharePoint/wiki access exercises ACLs and generates access logs; repos read and written; USB/printer/Bluetooth events happen occasionally, providing cover.

elements.label: Temporal structure, prompt response, and signal quality
elements.desc: Activity clusters in business hours with realistic tails; users click some phish and report others, approve expected MFA prompts and decline unexpected ones, dismiss some UAC/SmartScreen prompts; benign-activity diversity, entropy, and cross-user variance are measurable.

elements.label: Business-context lure plausibility
elements.desc: Decoys, deceptive credentials, decoy files, and honey-share content sit inside plausible surrounding business context so lures do not stand out as obvious traps.

elements.label: Process and script execution telemetry
elements.desc: Process creation with full command line, parent, hashes, integrity level (4688/Sysmon 1); image load, driver load; PowerShell ScriptBlock/Module/Transcript with deobfuscated content; AMSI verdicts; auditd execve.

elements.label: Authentication telemetry
elements.desc: Kerberos (4768/4769/4770/4771), NTLM validation (4776), logon (4624/4625) with full logon-type/source fields, account-mgmt (4720-4740); cross-system correlation so one logon produces matched events on client/DC/target.

elements.label: Object access: file, registry, and IPC
elements.desc: File create/write/delete/rename with audit (4663, Sysmon 11/23/26); registry set/delete with before/after value (4657, Sysmon 12/13/14); named-pipe and raw-access events; FIM baselines on critical paths.

elements.label: Network telemetry
elements.desc: Firewall/NGFW 5-tuple with bytes/duration/verdict; Zeek/Suricata/flow records with HTTP URI/host/UA and TLS SNI/JA3; DNS query/response logs with QNAME/QTYPE/resolver; netflow/IPFIX supporting volumetric analysis.

elements.label: Endpoint/EDR streams
elements.desc: API-hook telemetry for sensitive calls (VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory), LSASS access attempts with caller/target/mask, image-load with signer and memory-loaded indicators, memory-scan/YARA results.

elements.label: Identity-provider, cloud-plane, and application logs
elements.desc: IdP sign-in logs with conditional-access verdict and risk; CloudTrail/Azure Activity/GCP Audit with principal/action/resource; Kubernetes audit/admission; email tracking and mailbox audit; proxy/CASB with URL/user/bytes/category; app/DB audit with session/user/query.

elements.label: Schema fidelity, volume, and noise floor
elements.desc: Events conform to real schemas (EVTX, Sysmon, Zeek, CloudTrail) with full field population, typed/normalizable form (ECS/OCSF); volume reflects real fleet sizes; signal-to-noise realistic enough that naive rules produce realistic false positives; events generated as a byproduct of activity, not injected as oracle outputs.

elements.label: Coverage gaps, timing, and retention tiers
elements.desc: Coverage non-uniform (some hosts have Sysmon, some don't; agents go offline, shippers drop); events carry generation and ingestion timestamps with variable lag; hot/warm/cold retention differs by source.

elements.label: Integrity, enrichment, and cross-system correlation
elements.desc: Local log clearing doesn't remove SIEM evidence; sensor tampering produces heartbeat-loss telemetry; out-of-band channels survive endpoint compromise; events enriched with asset/user/geo/TI with stable entity resolution; joinable identifiers support cross-source correlation; defenders can deploy sensors, tune verbosity, author/backtest detections.
elements.anchors: T1070.001, T1685, T1588, UC-D5, UC-S1; D3-UBA, D3-MFA, D3-CE, D3-RA, D3-AMED

elements.label: Response outcome telemetry with explainable decisions
elements.desc: Defender response actions emit analyzable records (MFA results, credential changes, policy rollout, containment status, hardening, eviction, mediation, tamper indicators, restore validation) with timestamps/actors/targets/outcomes/provenance; allow/deny decisions record actor, resource, policy/rule, enforcement point, reason, result.
elements.anchors: D3-MFA, D3-PH, D3-CE, D3-PE, D3-RA, D3-RO, D3-NI, D3-AMED, D3-NTA, D3-UBA

elements.label: Recovery and deception telemetry
elements.desc: Recovery actions emit telemetry about source/provenance, integrity checks, restored object identity, and post-recovery success/failure; decoy interactions emit high-context records (first touch, attempted auth, accessed decoy object, follow-on movement) retained for investigation.

elements.label: Command fidelity
elements.desc: Real shells (PowerShell, cmd, Bash, Zsh, Python REPL) reachable as interactive or scripted sessions; commands execute against the real OS with real stdout/stderr, exit codes, pipes, redirects, subshells.

elements.label: API fidelity
elements.desc: Win32/NT, POSIX syscalls, cloud SDKs, Kubernetes API reachable from tooling with real effects and real error codes (ERROR_ACCESS_DENIED, EACCES, InvalidClientTokenId); kernel-side telemetry fires on the call.

elements.label: Parameter space
elements.desc: Exploits, lateral movement, discovery queries take real, typed, often large parameter spaces; wrong parameters produce wrong results (not automatic pass/fail); tool help is realistic.

elements.label: Failure realism
elements.desc: Failure is the deterministic consequence of a real mismatch, not a probability draw; authentication, authorization, and reachability are distinct failure modes; agents read error reasons and adapt.

elements.label: Composition, tool transfer, and interactive sessions
elements.desc: Multi-step operations composed by the agent, not invoked as super-actions; tools transferred via realistic channels (SMB, SCP, HTTP, Invoke-WebRequest) with size/time/telemetry cost; in-memory alternatives; sessions hold cwd/env/history.

elements.label: State effects, scheduling, and cost budgets
elements.desc: Actions that change state do so persistently and observably; side-effects on registry/FIM/process-create telemetry fire; destructive actions can't be trivially reverted; agents schedule via real primitives honoring delays; every action consumes time and generates telemetry.

elements.label: Observability, C2 semantics, and defender action
elements.desc: Agent sees the same output a human operator would; long-running commands stream incrementally; C2-dispatched actions have realistic latency/jitter/beacon cadence/queueing/cancellation; defender agents act through real tools (KQL/SPL queries, EDR isolation, firewall push, account disable) with propagation latency and rollback needs.
elements.anchors: T1071, T1104, UC-D2, UC-D4, UC-D5; D3-MFA, D3-CE, D3-PE, D3-RA, D3-RO, D3-NI, D3-AMED

elements.label: Privilege-scoped action surface and realistic tool-use idiom
elements.desc: Same command gives different output at different privilege levels; privilege changes (token, sudo, runas) alter the action surface in real time; the agent uses the full shell/API rather than a handcrafted menu, supporting evaluation with real tooling (Metasploit, Impacket, BloodHound, sqlmap).

elements.label: Distinct defender action families
elements.desc: The defender action space distinguishes hardening (MFA/integrity/allowlist), credential ops (revoke/reset/disable/unlock/reissue), process eviction (suspend/terminate/kill service/reboot/isolate), isolation and mediation (network isolation/host containment/session restriction/resource mediation), and restoration (file restore/reinstall/snapshot rollback/config rollback/integrity validation); collapsing these into one generic respond breaks evaluation.
elements.anchors: D3-MFA, D3-PH, D3-AMED, D3-CE, D3-RA, D3-PE, D3-NI, D3-RO

elements.label: Multi-step defender operations require ordered sequencing
elements.desc: Account recovery walks review then unlock then reissue then validation then restored access; file restoration walks source selection then rollback then integrity verification; abstract super-actions that skip intermediate states make the surface unevaluable.

elements.label: Position- and privilege-dependent observability
elements.desc: Visible traffic/hosts/sessions depend on network position (MITM only where topology allows); same query returns different detail at different privilege levels; administrative queries leave auth trails; pivoting changes the observation surface.

elements.label: Partial, incremental, scale-appropriate results
elements.desc: Enumerations paginate or throttle; large objects (user lists, BloodHound graphs, cloud inventory) require many queries; result volumes reflect enterprise scale so agents must parse and filter rather than pattern-match.

elements.label: Noise, uncertainty, and time-variance
elements.desc: Scans return closed/filtered/misleading responses; fingerprinting is probabilistic and banners can lie; stale entries look live; sessions end, caches expire; the agent cannot treat every returned datum as ground truth.

elements.label: Protocol, banner, and metadata fidelity on reception
elements.desc: Responses conform to real protocols (SMB error codes, HTTP headers, Kerberos error packets) with realistic timing and side-channels; host metadata consistent across paths; inconsistencies are real or attacker-induced, not simulator artifacts.

elements.label: Defender-side observation surface
elements.desc: Defender agents see events through a realistic interface (SIEM search, EDR console, SOAR queue) with real query syntax, pagination, latency; alerts carry timestamps/severity/source/asset/evidence; two layers (alerts vs raw events) with pivot; an analyst pivots from an anomalous login to related endpoint/service/file activity in one workflow.

elements.label: Blind spots and observation cost
elements.desc: Both sides reason about what they don't know; attacker can't see behind a firewall or read another user's files without escalation; defender can't see what sensors didn't capture; every observation is itself an action that generates telemetry, trading speed for visibility.

elements.label: Feedback loops and multi-source correlation
elements.desc: Action output informs the next observation and vice versa, enabling iterative probe-adapt; both sides may observe the same phenomenon from multiple sources that disagree, forcing reconciliation across sources.

elements.label: Oracle-free property
elements.desc: The agent is never handed ground-truth state; all evaluation-visible state must be derivable from observations the agent can make; no leakage through reward shape, action mask, or state vector; defenders reconstruct compromise status from alerts/sessions/traces/decoy touches rather than reading a true variable.

elements.label: Before/after response observation
elements.desc: Defender-facing views expose response outcomes comparable against prior visible state: after quarantine, reachability/policy/connection-failure events show whether containment took effect; after a restore, service-health and integrity validations show success; without before/after, 'fired' and 'worked' are indistinguishable.

elements.label: High-context deception observation
elements.desc: Decoy interactions surface through a coherent investigation view (first touch, source identity, decoy object, action sequence, follow-on movement, lateral activity) linked rather than scattered; a decoy dashboard or SIEM correlation makes the deception chain navigable.

elements.label: Authoritative DNS, WHOIS, and certificate transparency
elements.desc: External resolution with full record set, reverse DNS, WHOIS/RDAP with realistic registrants, passive-DNS history, CT logs revealing subdomains and staging hosts, OCSP/CRL reachable.

elements.label: OSINT surface: public web, social, repos, paste
elements.desc: Target public presence (site, jobs, filings), employee social footprint, breach-dump corpora, GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket with accidental secrets, Pastebin/Gist, search engines (Google, Shodan, Censys) returning realistic results.

elements.label: Threat intel, reputation, and external scanner observability
elements.desc: Domain/IP/hash reputation queryable by both sides with scores that update as infrastructure ages; external scanners (Shodan, Censys, GreyNoise) index exposed services so recon is itself observable by the defender.

elements.label: Attacker-controlled domains, hosting, and C2 servers
elements.desc: Domain registration (incl. typosquats/IDN homographs), VPS/bulletproof/compromised hosting with realistic cost and takedown latency; HTTP(S)/DNS/custom-protocol C2 with redirectors and multi-tier architectures; realistic TLS and categorization.

elements.label: Legitimate-service abuse for C2 blending
elements.desc: CDN domain fronting where historically possible, legitimate SaaS (Dropbox, GitHub, Slack, Discord, Telegram) reachable as covert-C2 channels, Tor and residential-proxy networks; legitimate categorization defeats naive detection.

elements.label: Attacker email and weaponized content hosting
elements.desc: Mail-sending infrastructure with configurable SPF/DKIM/DMARC on attacker domains; phishing pages, fake login portals, exploit-kit landings; malware staging; drive-by/watering-hole compromises reachable from simulated users.

elements.label: Compromised third parties and supply chain
elements.desc: Third-party websites, SaaS vendors, update servers as compromisable entities; vendor to MSP to target chains; upstream package registries publishable-to (typosquat, dependency confusion, compromised maintainer); CI/CD compromise paths.

elements.label: External identity and federation surface
elements.desc: Vendor/federated IdPs with realistic trust, OAuth illicit-consent ecosystem, external SAML/OIDC providers with forgeable assertions on key compromise, cross-tenant/cross-cloud boundaries modeled rather than collapsed.

elements.label: Full DNS resolution path, infrastructure lifecycle, and cost/attribution
elements.desc: stub-recursive-root-TLD-authoritative chain traversable (DNS hijack, DGA, tunneling); attacker infrastructure has a lifecycle (new-aging-used-burned) with reputation propagation; realistic monetary/temporal cost and attribution trails.
